## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Novel BUK mechanism — design judgment lives in the emitter shape; the engine change is additive and low-risk. RBK-ignorant: BUK provides mechanism only, names nothing of its own.
  
  ## Goal
  
  Give the zipper registry a stable cross-language symbol per enrolled colophon, and a generic emitter that projects name/value pairs to Rust consts — mirroring the existing bash-to-artifact pattern of `buz_emit_context`.
  
  ## Shape
  
  - `buz_enroll` must retain the enroll varname in a new roll. Today it `printf -v`'s the colophon into the caller and discards the varname, so the registry has no stable symbol for an emitter to name consts after. Additive: `printf -v` stays, existing callers untouched.
  - Add a generic Rust-const emitter (sibling target to `buz_emit_context`). DECIDED: the emitter is generic — it takes name/value pairs, not zipper rolls only. The later RBCC, fixture, and scattered-group projectors all feed this one mechanism. For the colophon case the pairs come from the kindled rolls with const name = enroll varname verbatim; other callers supply their own pairs.
  - The emitter emits `pub const NAME: &str = "...";` lines and is RBK-ignorant — it names nothing domain-specific itself.
  
  ## Sources
  
  - `Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh` — `buz_enroll` (varname discard site) and `buz_emit_context` (the bash-to-artifact template)
  
  ## What done looks like
  
  - A roll carries enroll varnames; a generic BUK emitter turns name/value pairs into `pub const NAME: &str = "...";` lines.
  - Acceptance is the emitter's own output: feed it the rbz rolls and confirm the emitted consts match the expected colophons. The BUK self-test is a no-regression check, not emitter verification.

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BP

## Shape

Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.

The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, fixture names, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work builds one generic bash→Rust const emitter and points every co-maintained group through it.

## Locked decisions

- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
- **Emitter is generic.** One BUK mechanism takes name/value pairs and emits Rust consts; every projector (colophon, RBCC, fixture, scattered) reuses it rather than each owning bespoke codegen.
- **Full co-maintained set is in scope** — not just colophons + moorings dir. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc), fixture names (rbte). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earn a canonical-author tidy first (give them one bash home), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.

## What done looks like

The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The rbtd manifest's hand-mirrored groups and the lib.rs path macros are fed from generation; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate.

# jjezs_pace ₢BPAAC

## Character

Novel BUK mechanism — design judgment lives in the emitter shape; the engine change is additive and low-risk. RBK-ignorant: BUK provides mechanism only, names nothing of its own.

## Goal

Give the zipper registry a stable cross-language symbol per enrolled colophon, and a generic emitter that projects name/value pairs to Rust consts — mirroring the existing bash-to-artifact pattern of `buz_emit_context`.

## Shape

- `buz_enroll` must retain the enroll varname in a new roll. Today it `printf -v`'s the colophon into the caller and discards the varname, so the registry has no stable symbol for an emitter to name consts after. Additive: `printf -v` stays, existing callers untouched.
- Add a generic Rust-const emitter (sibling target to `buz_emit_context`). DECIDED: the emitter is generic — it takes name/value pairs, not zipper rolls only. The later RBCC, fixture, and scattered-group projectors all feed this one mechanism. For the colophon case the pairs come from the kindled rolls with const name = enroll varname verbatim; other callers supply their own pairs.
- The emitter emits `pub const NAME: &str = "...";` lines and is RBK-ignorant — it names nothing domain-specific itself.

## Sources

- `Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh` — `buz_enroll` (varname discard site) and `buz_emit_context` (the bash-to-artifact template)

## What done looks like

- A roll carries enroll varnames; a generic BUK emitter turns name/value pairs into `pub const NAME: &str = "...";` lines.
- Acceptance is the emitter's own output: feed it the rbz rolls and confirm the emitted consts match the expected colophons. The BUK self-test is a no-regression check, not emitter verification.

-rw-r--r--@ 1 bhyslop  staff  10160 May  3 12:40 Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh
exit=0

28	# Internal kindle boilerplate
29	
30	zbuz_kindle() {
31	  test -z "${ZBUZ_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "buz already kindled"
32	
33	  # Registry rolls (populated by buz_enroll in consumer kindle, same-process only)
34	  z_buz_colophon_roll=()
35	  z_buz_module_roll=()
36	  z_buz_command_roll=()
37	  z_buz_channel_roll=()
38	  z_buz_describe_roll=()
39	
40	  # Group rolls (populated by buz_group, consumed by buz_emit_context)
41	  z_buz_group_index_roll=()
42	  z_buz_group_prefix_roll=()
43	  z_buz_group_description_roll=()
44	
45	  readonly ZBUZ_KINDLED=1
46	}
47	
48	######################################################################
49	# Internal sentinel
50	
51	zbuz_sentinel() {
52	  test "${ZBUZ_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module buz not kindled - call zbuz_kindle first"
53	}
54	
55	######################################################################
56	# Internal helpers
57	
58	# zbuz_resolve_tabtarget_capture() - Resolve colophon to tabtarget path
59	# Args: colophon
60	# Returns: tabtarget path or exit 1
61	zbuz_resolve_tabtarget_capture() {
62	  zbuz_sentinel
63	
64	  local z_colophon="${1:-}"
65	  test -n "${z_colophon}" || return 1
66	
67	  local z_matches=("${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR}/${z_colophon}."*.sh)
68	
69	  # Bash 3.2: no-match glob returns literal — check with test -e
70	  test -e "${z_matches[0]}" || return 1
71	
72	  # Allow multiple matches (imprinted colophons share a colophon prefix)
73	  # Return first match as representative
74	  echo "${z_matches[0]}"
75	}
76	
77	######################################################################
78	# Group declaration (category metadata for context generation)
79	
80	# buz_group() - Declare a colophon group category
81	# Args: constant_name, prefix, description
82	# Stores group metadata at the current roll position for buz_emit_context.
83	buz_group() {
84	  zbuz_sentinel
85	
86	  local -r z_constant="${1:-}"
87	  local -r z_prefix="${2:-}"
88	  local -r z_description="${3:-}"
89	  test -n "${z_constant}"    || buc_die "buz_group: constant name required"
90	  test -n "${z_prefix}"      || buc_die "buz_group: prefix required"
91	  test -n "${z_description}" || buc_die "buz_group: description required"
92	
93	  z_buz_group_index_roll+=("${#z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}")
94	  z_buz_group_prefix_roll+=("${z_prefix}")
95	  z_buz_group_description_roll+=("${z_description}")
96	}
97	
98	######################################################################
99	# Public enroll (kindle-only registry population)
100	
101	# buz_enroll() - Register colophon tuple in parallel rolls
102	# Args: varname, colophon, module, command, channel, description
103	# All 6 arguments required. Channel: "" (none), "imprint", or "param1".
104	# Assigns colophon string to caller's variable via printf -v
105	# Side effects: populates registry rolls (must be called in same process, NOT inside $())
106	buz_enroll() {
107	  zbuz_sentinel
108	
109	  test $# -eq 6 || buc_die "buz_enroll: requires 6 arguments (varname colophon module command channel description), got $#"
110	
111	  local -r z_varname="${1}"
112	  local -r z_colophon="${2}"
113	  local -r z_module="${3}"
114	  local -r z_command="${4}"
115	  local -r z_channel="${5}"
116	  local -r z_description="${6}"
117	  test -n "${z_varname}"     || buc_die "buz_enroll: varname required"
118	  test -n "${z_colophon}"    || buc_die "buz_enroll: colophon required"
119	  test -n "${z_module}"      || buc_die "buz_enroll: module required"
120	  test -n "${z_command}"     || buc_die "buz_enroll: command required"
121	  test -n "${z_description}" || buc_die "buz_enroll: description required"
122	
123	  # Validate channel value (empty string is valid — means no channel)
124	  case "${z_channel}" in
125	    ""|"imprint"|"param1") ;;
126	    *) buc_die "buz_enroll: invalid channel: ${z_channel}" ;;
127	  esac
128	
129	  # Validate variable name
130	  [[ "${z_varname}" =~ ^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$ ]] \
131	    || buc_die "buz_enroll: invalid variable name: ${z_varname}"
132	
133	  # Roll population (only persists in same-process context, lost in $() subshell)
134	  z_buz_colophon_roll+=("${z_colophon}")
135	  z_buz_module_roll+=("${z_module}")
136	  z_buz_command_roll+=("${z_command}")
137	  z_buz_channel_roll+=("${z_channel}")
138	  z_buz_describe_roll+=("${z_description}")
139	
140	  # Assign colophon to caller's variable
141	  printf -v "${z_varname}" '%s' "${z_colophon}" || buc_die "buz_enroll: printf -v failed for ${z_varname}"
142	}
143	
144	######################################################################
145	# Context generation (emit markdown from registry metadata)
146	
147	# buz_emit_context() - Emit colophon registry as markdown, organized by groups
148	# Args: tabtarget_dir
149	# Writes markdown to stdout. Groups come from buz_group; descriptions from buz_enroll.
150	# Frontispiece extracted from tabtarget filenames on disk.
151	buz_emit_context() {
152	  zbuz_sentinel
153	
154	  local -r z_tt_dir="${1:-}"
155	  test -n "${z_tt_dir}" || buc_die "buz_emit_context: tabtarget directory required"
156	
157	  printf '%s\n' "## Command Reference (Generated)"
158	  printf '%s\n' ""
159	  printf '%s\n' "<!-- Generated by buz_emit_context from zipper registry. Do not edit. -->"
160	  printf '%s\n' "<!-- Regenerate: tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh -->"
161	  printf '%s\n' ""
162	  printf '%s\n' "**Folio** is the runtime target value passed to a command (nameplate moniker, role name, etc.)."
163	  printf '%s\n' "The Folio column shows how each tabtarget receives it:"
164	  printf '%s\n' ""
165	  printf '%s\n' "- **imprint**: Folio is baked into the filename — one tabtarget per target (e.g., \`tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh\`)"
166	  printf '%s\n' "- **param1**: Folio is passed as a command-line argument (e.g., \`tt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh tadmor\`)"
167	  printf '%s\n' "- **—**: No folio needed — standalone command"
168	  printf '%s\n' ""
169	
170	  local z_group_cursor=0
171	  local z_in_table=0
172	  local z_i
173	
174	  for z_i in "${!z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}"; do
175	    # Emit group header when we reach its starting index
176	    if (( z_group_cursor < ${#z_buz_group_index_roll[@]} )) \
177	       && (( z_i == z_buz_group_index_roll[z_group_cursor] )); then
178	      if (( z_in_table )); then
179	        printf '%s\n' ""
180	      fi
181	      printf '### %s (`%s`)\n' \
182	        "${z_buz_group_description_roll[z_group_cursor]}" \
183	        "${z_buz_group_prefix_roll[z_group_cursor]}"
184	      printf '%s\n' ""
185	      printf '%s\n' "| Colophon | Frontispiece | Folio | Purpose |"
186	      printf '%s\n' "|----------|-------------|-------|---------|"
187	      z_in_table=1
188	      z_group_cursor=$((z_group_cursor + 1))
189	    fi
190	
191	    # Resolve frontispiece from tabtarget filename
192	    local z_colophon="${z_buz_colophon_roll[z_i]}"
193	    local z_frontispiece=""
194	    local z_matches=("${z_tt_dir}/${z_colophon}."*.sh)
195	    if test -e "${z_matches[0]}"; then
196	      local z_basename="${z_matches[0]##*/}"
197	      local z_stem="${z_basename%.sh}"
198	      local z_skip=$(( ${#z_colophon} + 1 ))
199	      local z_after="${z_stem:z_skip}"
200	      z_frontispiece="${z_after%%.*}"
201	    fi
202	
203	    local z_channel="${z_buz_channel_roll[z_i]:-}"
204	    local z_folio_display="${z_channel:-—}"
205	    local z_desc="${z_buz_describe_roll[z_i]:-}"
206	    printf '| `%s` | %s | %s | %s |\n' "${z_colophon}" "${z_frontispiece}" "${z_folio_display}" "${z_desc}"
207	  done
208	
209	  if (( z_in_table )); then
210	    printf '%s\n' ""
211	  fi
212	}
213	
214	######################################################################
215	# Healthcheck (opt-in tabtarget validation — called by consumer, not by BUK)
216	
217	# buz_healthcheck() - Validate that all enrolled colophons have tabtargets on disk
218	# Dies on first missing tabtarget. Call after enrollment is complete.
219	buz_healthcheck() {
220	  zbuz_sentinel
221	
222	  local z_i=""
223	  for z_i in "${!z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}"; do
224	    zbuz_resolve_tabtarget_capture "${z_buz_colophon_roll[z_i]}" >/dev/null \
225	      || buc_die "buz_healthcheck: no tabtarget for colophon '${z_buz_colophon_roll[z_i]}' in ${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR}/"
226	  done
227	}

# Internal kindle boilerplate

zbuz_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZBUZ_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "buz already kindled"

  # Registry rolls (populated by buz_enroll in consumer kindle, same-process only)
  z_buz_colophon_roll=()
  z_buz_module_roll=()
  z_buz_command_roll=()
  z_buz_channel_roll=()
  z_buz_describe_roll=()

  # Group rolls (populated by buz_group, consumed by buz_emit_context)
  z_buz_group_index_roll=()
  z_buz_group_prefix_roll=()
  z_buz_group_description_roll=()

  readonly ZBUZ_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Internal sentinel

zbuz_sentinel() {
  test "${ZBUZ_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module buz not kindled - call zbuz_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

# zbuz_resolve_tabtarget_capture() - Resolve colophon to tabtarget path
# Args: colophon
# Returns: tabtarget path or exit 1
zbuz_resolve_tabtarget_capture() {
  zbuz_sentinel

  local z_colophon="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_colophon}" || return 1

  local z_matches=("${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR}/${z_colophon}."*.sh)

  # Bash 3.2: no-match glob returns literal — check with test -e
  test -e "${z_matches[0]}" || return 1

  # Allow multiple matches (imprinted colophons share a colophon prefix)
  # Return first match as representative
  echo "${z_matches[0]}"
}

######################################################################
# Group declaration (category metadata for context generation)

# buz_group() - Declare a colophon group category
# Args: constant_name, prefix, description
# Stores group metadata at the current roll position for buz_emit_context.
buz_group() {
  zbuz_sentinel

  local -r z_constant="${1:-}"
  local -r z_prefix="${2:-}"
  local -r z_description="${3:-}"
  test -n "${z_constant}"    || buc_die "buz_group: constant name required"
  test -n "${z_prefix}"      || buc_die "buz_group: prefix required"
  test -n "${z_description}" || buc_die "buz_group: description required"

  z_buz_group_index_roll+=("${#z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}")
  z_buz_group_prefix_roll+=("${z_prefix}")
  z_buz_group_description_roll+=("${z_description}")
}

######################################################################
# Public enroll (kindle-only registry population)

# buz_enroll() - Register colophon tuple in parallel rolls
# Args: varname, colophon, module, command, channel, description
# All 6 arguments required. Channel: "" (none), "imprint", or "param1".
# Assigns colophon string to caller's variable via printf -v
# Side effects: populates registry rolls (must be called in same process, NOT inside $())
buz_enroll() {
  zbuz_sentinel

  test $# -eq 6 || buc_die "buz_enroll: requires 6 arguments (varname colophon module command channel description), got $#"

  local -r z_varname="${1}"
  local -r z_colophon="${2}"
  local -r z_module="${3}"
  local -r z_command="${4}"
  local -r z_channel="${5}"
  local -r z_description="${6}"
  test -n "${z_varname}"     || buc_die "buz_enroll: varname required"
  test -n "${z_colophon}"    || buc_die "buz_enroll: colophon required"
  test -n "${z_module}"      || buc_die "buz_enroll: module required"
  test -n "${z_command}"     || buc_die "buz_enroll: command required"
  test -n "${z_description}" || buc_die "buz_enroll: description required"

  # Validate channel value (empty string is valid — means no channel)
  case "${z_channel}" in
    ""|"imprint"|"param1") ;;
    *) buc_die "buz_enroll: invalid channel: ${z_channel}" ;;
  esac

  # Validate variable name
  [[ "${z_varname}" =~ ^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$ ]] \
    || buc_die "buz_enroll: invalid variable name: ${z_varname}"

  # Roll population (only persists in same-process context, lost in $() subshell)
  z_buz_colophon_roll+=("${z_colophon}")
  z_buz_module_roll+=("${z_module}")
  z_buz_command_roll+=("${z_command}")
  z_buz_channel_roll+=("${z_channel}")
  z_buz_describe_roll+=("${z_description}")

  # Assign colophon to caller's variable
  printf -v "${z_varname}" '%s' "${z_colophon}" || buc_die "buz_enroll: printf -v failed for ${z_varname}"
}

######################################################################
# Context generation (emit markdown from registry metadata)

# buz_emit_context() - Emit colophon registry as markdown, organized by groups
# Args: tabtarget_dir
# Writes markdown to stdout. Groups come from buz_group; descriptions from buz_enroll.
# Frontispiece extracted from tabtarget filenames on disk.
buz_emit_context() {
  zbuz_sentinel

  local -r z_tt_dir="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_tt_dir}" || buc_die "buz_emit_context: tabtarget directory required"

  printf '%s\n' "## Command Reference (Generated)"
  printf '%s\n' ""
  printf '%s\n' "<!-- Generated by buz_emit_context from zipper registry. Do not edit. -->"
  printf '%s\n' "<!-- Regenerate: tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh -->"
  printf '%s\n' ""
  printf '%s\n' "**Folio** is the runtime target value passed to a command (nameplate moniker, role name, etc.)."
  printf '%s\n' "The Folio column shows how each tabtarget receives it:"
  printf '%s\n' ""
  printf '%s\n' "- **imprint**: Folio is baked into the filename — one tabtarget per target (e.g., \`tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh\`)"
  printf '%s\n' "- **param1**: Folio is passed as a command-line argument (e.g., \`tt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh tadmor\`)"
  printf '%s\n' "- **—**: No folio needed — standalone command"
  printf '%s\n' ""

  local z_group_cursor=0
  local z_in_table=0
  local z_i

  for z_i in "${!z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}"; do
    # Emit group header when we reach its starting index
    if (( z_group_cursor < ${#z_buz_group_index_roll[@]} )) \
       && (( z_i == z_buz_group_index_roll[z_group_cursor] )); then
      if (( z_in_table )); then
        printf '%s\n' ""
      fi
      printf '### %s (`%s`)\n' \
        "${z_buz_group_description_roll[z_group_cursor]}" \
        "${z_buz_group_prefix_roll[z_group_cursor]}"
      printf '%s\n' ""
      printf '%s\n' "| Colophon | Frontispiece | Folio | Purpose |"
      printf '%s\n' "|----------|-------------|-------|---------|"
      z_in_table=1
      z_group_cursor=$((z_group_cursor + 1))
    fi

    # Resolve frontispiece from tabtarget filename
    local z_colophon="${z_buz_colophon_roll[z_i]}"
    local z_frontispiece=""
    local z_matches=("${z_tt_dir}/${z_colophon}."*.sh)
    if test -e "${z_matches[0]}"; then
      local z_basename="${z_matches[0]##*/}"
      local z_stem="${z_basename%.sh}"
      local z_skip=$(( ${#z_colophon} + 1 ))
      local z_after="${z_stem:z_skip}"
      z_frontispiece="${z_after%%.*}"
    fi

    local z_channel="${z_buz_channel_roll[z_i]:-}"
    local z_folio_display="${z_channel:-—}"
    local z_desc="${z_buz_describe_roll[z_i]:-}"
    printf '| `%s` | %s | %s | %s |\n' "${z_colophon}" "${z_frontispiece}" "${z_folio_display}" "${z_desc}"
  done

  if (( z_in_table )); then
    printf '%s\n' ""
  fi
}

######################################################################
# Healthcheck (opt-in tabtarget validation — called by consumer, not by BUK)

# buz_healthcheck() - Validate that all enrolled colophons have tabtargets on disk
# Dies on first missing tabtarget. Call after enrollment is complete.
buz_healthcheck() {
  zbuz_sentinel

  local z_i=""
  for z_i in "${!z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}"; do
    zbuz_resolve_tabtarget_capture "${z_buz_colophon_roll[z_i]}" >/dev/null \
      || buc_die "buz_healthcheck: no tabtarget for colophon '${z_buz_colophon_roll[z_i]}' in ${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR}/"
  done
}

content.text:
## Mounting ₣BP → ₢BPAAC (buz-rust-const-emitter) `[rough]`

**Pace goal:** Give the zipper registry a stable cross-language symbol per enrolled colophon, then add a generic BUK emitter that turns name/value pairs into Rust `pub const NAME: &str = "...";` lines — the Rust-side mirror of the existing `buz_emit_context` markdown emitter.

**Heat context** — `rbk-13-mvp-redundancy-cleanup` (₣BP), 2/8 done. Shape: consolidate co-maintained constant groups to a single canonical bash author, then project each through *one* generic bash→Rust emitter (retiring the runtime drift check for a build-time diff gate). This pace builds the emitter mechanism that all later projectors (RBCC, fixtures, scattered groups) feed.

**Grounding** (read `Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh`):
- `zbuz_kindle` (line 30) declares five parallel registry rolls; `buz_enroll` (106) appends to them and `printf -v`'s the colophon into the caller, **discarding `z_varname`** — exactly the gap the docket names.
- `buz_emit_context` (151) is the template: `zbuz_sentinel`, iterate rolls, `printf` to stdout. The Rust emitter is its sibling.

**Proposed approach** (2 edits, one file, additive):
1. Add a `z_buz_varname_roll` to `zbuz_kindle` and append `${z_varname}` in `buz_enroll`. `printf -v` and all existing callers stay untouched.
2. Add a generic `buz_emit_rust_consts` that emits `pub const NAME: &str = "...";` from name/value pairs and names nothing RBK-specific. For the colophon case the pairs are (varname roll, colophon roll).

**One design decision the docket leaves open** — *how* the generic emitter receives its name/value pairs. This is where the judgment lives:

questions.question: How should the generic Rust-const emitter receive its name/value pairs?

options.label: Two named array args (nameref-style)
options.description: Caller passes two parallel array variable names; emitter reads them via indirection (mirrors how the rolls already live as bash arrays). Colophon caller passes varname-roll + colophon-roll directly. Most idiomatic to existing roll design.
options.preview:
buz_emit_rust_consts NAMES_ARRAY VALUES_ARRAY
# emitter does: eval / ${!ref} to read pairs

# colophon caller:
buz_emit_rust_consts \
  z_buz_varname_roll z_buz_colophon_roll

options.label: Pairs on stdin
options.description: Caller pipes `NAME<TAB>VALUE` lines; emitter reads stdin and emits a const per line. Fully decoupled from bash arrays — any producer works. Loosest coupling, but loses array-ness.
options.preview:
printf '%s\t%s\n' \
  RBW_CC rbw-cC ... \
  | buz_emit_rust_consts

# emitter: while read name value; do
#   printf 'pub const %s...' ...
# done

options.label: Variadic alternating args
options.description: Caller passes name1 value1 name2 value2 ...; emitter walks args two at a time. Simple, no indirection, but awkward for the roll case (must expand both arrays interleaved).

1	<!--
2	Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
3	SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
4	See LICENSE for terms.
5	-->
6	
7	# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference
8	
9	## Core Philosophy
10	
11	This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.
12	
13	The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.
14	
15	Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.
16	
17	Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.
18	
19	The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.
20	
21	### Load-Bearing Complexity
22	
23	Every element in a system — every function extraction, every pattern variant, every structural distinction — must carry weight. An element is **load-bearing** when its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, ask whether the difference is load-bearing: if yes, document why; if no, homogenize.
24	
25	This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" — load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"
26	
27	Concrete examples from this codebase:
28	
29	- **Four separate `rbgi_add_*_iam` functions** look like copy-paste, but each wraps a different GCP API with different failure modes. The variation is load-bearing — extracting a "generic IAM helper" would hide which API failed at 3am.
30	- **Three copy-pasted Secret Manager IAM blocks** are NOT load-bearing variation — same API, same failure class, same recovery. Extract a helper.
31	- **Not adding three-part structure to a simple `api_enable` spec definition** — the structure would add ceremony without carrying meaning. Non-load-bearing elements increase cognitive cost without increasing correctness.
32	
33	Non-load-bearing elements are the silent budget overruns of a codebase. Each one is small; their aggregate is what makes systems incomprehensible.
34	
35	## Module Architecture
36	
37	Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.
38	
39	```bash
40	«prefix»_«name».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
41	«prefix»_cli.sh (entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
42	```
43	
44	### CLI as Module Gateway
45	
46	**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
47	
48	**Pattern**:
49	```
50	External Code → CLI script → furnish() kindles → module functions
51	```
52	
53	**Why this matters**:
54	- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
55	- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
56	- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
57	- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality
58	
59	**Anti-pattern**:
60	```bash
61	# ❌ External script kindling directly
62	source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
63	zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
64	rbob_charge
65	```
66	
67	**Correct pattern**:
68	```bash
69	# ✅ External script delegates to CLI
70	exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_charge "$@"
71	```
72	
73	**What counts as "external code"**:
74	- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
75	- TabTargets and launchers
76	- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
77	- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules
78	
79	**What can call kindle**:
80	- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
81	- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
82	- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior
83	
84	**Filesystem marker**: The `_cli.sh` suffix and the executable bit must agree. Within a BCG module directory:
85	
86	- Every `*_cli.sh` MUST be executable
87	- Every other `*.sh` MUST NOT be executable
88	
89	This makes the gateway role visible to `ls -l`: a workbench, testbench, or implementation module carrying `+x` is a bug — direct execution bypasses the CLI's `furnish()` and the kindle graph it owns. Tabtargets in `tt/` and shipped tool binaries are not BCG modules and stand outside this rule.
90	
91	### Module-CLI Prefix Unity
92	
93	**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).
94	
95	When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
96	only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
97	function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
98	dependencies based on which command is being invoked.
99	
100	### Regime Module Archetype
101	
102	A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields — the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:
103	
104	| Component | Location | Purpose |
105	|-----------|----------|---------|
106	| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
107	| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
108	| `z«prefix»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
109	| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
110	| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` — explained per-field display |
111	| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` — diagnostic dump |
112	
113	Furnish sequence: kindle → enforce → lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.
114	
115	**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.
116	
117	Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.
118	
119	---
120	
121	## Function Patterns
122	
123	### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)
124	
125	| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
126	|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
127	| `z«prefix»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
128	| `z«prefix»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
129	| `z«prefix»_enforce`  | Implementation | `z«prefix»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
130	| `z«prefix»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
131	| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
132	| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (buc_command)  | N/A               | Source buc_command.sh only                       |
133	
134	**Kindle constant**: Any variable — internal (`Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) — defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function — including enroll, setup, or helper functions — may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
135	
136	**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.
137	
138	**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must resolve to pure string literals at source time — **no runtime dependency, no `$(…)` computation, no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables**. Tinder-on-tinder expansion is permitted: a tinder value may interpolate `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` for any tinder constant defined earlier in the same module's tinder block, since that expansion resolves at source time and preserves the no-runtime-dependency invariant. They are available immediately after sourcing — no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling — inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.
139	
140	**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.
141	
142	### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)
143	
144	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
145	
146	| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
147	|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
148	| Public          | `«prefix»_«command»` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
149	| Internal helper | `z«prefix»_«name»`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |

155	### Template 1: Shebang and Copyright

157	```bash
158	#!/bin/bash
159	# shellcheck disable=SC2153  # kindle chain - per BCG
160	#
161	# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
162	#
163	# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
164	# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
