## Module Maturity Checklist

### Module Structure
- [ ] Implementation file «prefix»_«name».sh exists
- [ ] CLI file «prefix»_cli.sh exists (skip if library/utility module with no direct user commands)
- [ ] Implementation has multiple inclusion detection guard (`Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED`)
- [ ] CLI starts with `set -euo pipefail`
- [ ] Implementation file sources nothing (except within kindle function)
- [ ] CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other deps sourced in furnish
- [ ] External code accesses module through CLI only (no direct `z*_kindle()` calls from outside)
- [ ] `_cli.sh` files are executable; all other `*.sh` files in the module directory are not

### Required Functions
- [ ] `z«prefix»_kindle` - first line: `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die`
- [ ] `z«prefix»_sentinel` - first line: `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die`
- [ ] `z«prefix»_furnish` (CLI only) - documents env vars, sources all deps, calls kindle; gates with `buc_doc_env_done`
- [ ] `z«prefix»_enforce` (regime archetype only) - `buv_vet` + custom format checks after kindle
- [ ] All public functions start with sentinel check
- [ ] All internal helpers prefixed with `z«prefix»_`

### Variable Management
- [ ] All kindle constants (internal `Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING` and public `«PREFIX»_SCREAMING`) defined exclusively in kindle with `readonly`
- [ ] No kindle constant assignments outside kindle function
- [ ] Mutable kindle state (counters, rolls, builder state) uses lowercase `z_«prefix»_name` — no `readonly`
- [ ] Tinder constants (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) resolve at source time — pure literals or `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` expansion of earlier tinder from the same module; no `$(…)` computation; no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables; placed after `SOURCED` guard
- [ ] All local variables use `z_` prefix
- [ ] All expansions use `"${var}"` pattern (braced, quoted)
- [ ] Parameters use `"${1:-}"` pattern for defensive programming
- [ ] Module state variable `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` is the last statement in kindle
- [ ] No bare `$var` or unbraced `"$var"` expansions
- [ ] No tilde in quoted strings — use `${HOME}` instead
- [ ] No `local -i` — use plain local with explicit validation
- [ ] No raw `eval` for value assignment — use `printf -v` after name validation; `${!name}` for reading

### Error Handling
- [ ] Every command that can fail has `|| buc_die` (`|| buc_warn` only with a human-authored comment granting permission and explaining why non-fatal is safe)
- [ ] `_predicate` functions return 0/1, never die, no output
- [ ] `_capture` functions output once at end or exit 1, no stderr
- [ ] `_enroll` functions set `z_«funcname»_«retval»` return vars, never echo; callers never use `$()` (when applicable)
- [ ] `_enroll` functions called only within kindle (when applicable)
- [ ] `_recite` functions never mutate roll arrays (when applicable)
- [ ] Roll arrays use `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll` naming convention (when applicable)
- [ ] Two-line pattern for capturing: `z_var=$(func_capture) || buc_die`
- [ ] File reads validated: `test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die`
- [ ] Pipelines either: occur inside a `_capture` function, write to temp files with explicit status checks, or explicitly inspect `${PIPESTATUS[@]}`
- [ ] No `test $(command)` — use `grep -q` or `case` for validation
- [ ] Only `_predicate` functions in `if`/`while` conditions — no regular/enroll functions in conditionals
- [ ] No `test ... && break/continue/return` — invert test and use `||`
- [ ] Error blocks use `{ ...; }` not `( ... )` — no `|| (... exit ...)` patterns
- [ ] Isolation subshells (`( ... ) || buc_die`) have `|| buc_die` on every internal command and on the outer boundary

### Command Substitution Rules
- [ ] NO `local z_var=$(cmd)` — always two-line pattern (`local` swallows exit status)
- [ ] NO pipelines inside `$()` — use `_capture` function or temp files
- [ ] NO unguarded `$()` — every `$(cmd)` must have `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`
- [ ] NO `$()` on external commands — use temp files; `_capture`/`_recite` for contracted functions only
- [ ] Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) permitted in `$()` — no file-based alternative
- [ ] `$(<file)` always followed by validation
- [ ] Temp files for all external command output capture
- [ ] `_capture` and `_recite` functions properly named with suffix
- [ ] `_yawp` functions properly named with suffix

### Yawp Functions (when applicable)
- [ ] `_yawp` functions use pure bash builtins only — no external commands
- [ ] Return variable `z_«module»_«group»` initialized in kindle as mutable kindle state
- [ ] All `_yawp` functions in a group share one return variable
- [ ] Every yawp call immediately followed by `local -r z_name="${z_«module»_«group»}"` — no exceptions
- [ ] Return variable never appears directly in output calls (`buh_line`, `printf`, etc.)
- [ ] No `|| buc_die` at call site (cannot fail)
- [ ] No `buc_die` internally
- [ ] No stdout or stderr output

### Loop Safety
- [ ] All while-read loops use load-then-iterate pattern
- [ ] All `while read` conditions include trailing-newline guard: `|| test -n "${z_var}"`
- [ ] No file descriptor held open across function calls that may touch stdin

### Bash Compatibility
- [ ] No bash 4+ features (associative arrays, `**`, readarray)
- [ ] Use `test` not `[[ ]]` except for pattern matching `=~`
- [ ] Use `=` not `==` in test expressions
- [ ] No HERE documents (heredocs)
- [ ] Here-strings (`<<<`) single-line piping only; no multi-line
- [ ] Target bash 3.2 minimum
- [ ] Array iteration uses index pattern for `set -u` safety

### Naming Conventions
- [ ] Module prefix: 2-4 lowercase letters + underscore
- [ ] Public functions: `«prefix»_«command»` (snake_case)
- [ ] Internal functions: `z«prefix»_«name»` (snake_case)
- [ ] Internal constants: `Z«PREFIX»_«NAME»` (SCREAMING_SNAKE)
- [ ] Public constants: `«PREFIX»_«NAME»` (SCREAMING_SNAKE)
- [ ] Special functions: `*_predicate`, `*_capture`, `*_enroll`, `*_recite` suffixes

### Documentation & Visibility
- [ ] Public functions documented with `buc_doc_*` blocks
- [ ] Internal functions documented with `buc_doc_*` blocks (recommended)
- [ ] `buc_doc_shown || return 0` after documentation in functions that have it
- [ ] Runtime info via `buc_log_«source»` (forensic) and `buc_step` (milestones), never comments
- [ ] Details use `buc_log_«source»` (transcript.txt)
- [ ] Comments only for code clarification, not runtime info
- [ ] No commit-message comments (narrating the change, not the code)
- [ ] Major operations use `buc_step` (always visible)

### Sourcing Restrictions
- [ ] CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`
- [ ] `z«prefix»_furnish` sources all other dependencies and config files
- [ ] Furnish gates with `buc_doc_env_done || return 0` before any sourcing
- [ ] Credential sourcing documented and never writes to disk
- [ ] No other sourcing anywhere

### Interface Contamination Discipline
- [ ] Commands accept exactly one canonical form for each argument — no aliases
- [ ] No case folding, prefix stripping, or normalization of inputs
- [ ] No `${1:-default}` for required parameters — missing required args cause `buc_die`
- [ ] `buc_doc_*` output shows the single canonical invocation form
- [ ] Zeroes theory applied: no unexplained state space expansion

### Code Quality
- [ ] Prefer bash builtins over external tools (parameter expansion vs sed/awk)
- [ ] Use BCU utilities instead of raw bash (buc_die vs echo+exit)
- [ ] Use enrollment for config validation, `buv_dir_exists`/`buv_file_exists` for path checks
- [ ] Consistent error messages (specific and actionable)
- [ ] Proper temp file naming with module prefix
- [ ] No silent failures or ignored conditions
- [ ] No `2>/dev/null` — stderr redirected to temp file, path included in `buc_die` message
- [ ] Temp files never deleted in module code — preserved for forensics

### Enterprise Safety
- [ ] Crash-fast principle applied throughout
- [ ] No elegant-but-fragile pipeline patterns
- [ ] Secrets never written to disk (use `_capture` functions)
- [ ] Temp files make failures visible and debuggable
- [ ] Every potential error explicitly handled
- [ ] Abstraction layers used (BCU utilities, enrollment infrastructure)

### Regime Archetype (when applicable)
- [ ] Kindle calls `buv_*_enroll` for each config field
- [ ] Kindle calls `buv_scope_sentinel` after all enrollments, before `KINDLED=1`
- [ ] `z«prefix»_enforce` calls `buv_vet SCOPE` then custom format checks
- [ ] Furnish calls kindle → enforce (ironclad gate)
- [ ] CLI provides `*_validate` command using `buv_report`
- [ ] CLI provides `*_render` command using `buv_render`

---

## Test Execution Patterns

### Principled Subshell Use

BCG prohibits subshells for error handling (see **Subshell Exit vs Brace-Group**). Test execution is the principled counterpoint: subshells provide **isolation boundaries** that prevent state leakage between fixtures and cases.

In bash 3.2, there is no module system, no scope isolation, and no cleanup-on-exit guarantee. When fixture A kindles modules and fixture B kindles the same modules, the kindle guards (`Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED`) from fixture A block fixture B. Subshells solve this: all state — kindle guards, sourced functions, variables — dies at the `)` boundary.

This is discipline encoded as structure.

### Three-Layer Model

Test execution uses three layers separated by two subshell boundaries:

```
Parent Shell (runner layer)
 ├─ Fixture iteration and status tracking
 └─ Reporting (pass/fail/skip counts)
     │
     └─ Fixture Subshell (fixture boundary)
         ├─ _baste — kindle, source, configure
         ├─ Fixture temp dir creation
         └─ Case iteration
             │
             └─ Case Subshell (_tcase boundary)
                 ├─ Per-case BUT_TEMP_DIR and BUTE_BURV_ROOT
                 ├─ Assertions and verification
                 └─ Exit status communicated to fixture
```

### Vocabulary

**Fixture boundary** — the fixture isolation boundary:
- Runs in a subshell (state dies at boundary)
- Baste runs inside (visible to cases, dies with fixture)
- Communicates only exit status to the runner
- Kindle guards catch double-kindle within a fixture; subshell boundary prevents cross-fixture contamination

**`_tcase`** — the case isolation boundary:
- Runs in a subshell within the fixture subshell
- Inherits baste state, cannot mutate sibling state
- Communicates only exit status and stdio
- Each case gets isolated temp dir and BURV root

**`_baste`** — fixture preparation function (fixture layer):
- Runs inside the fixture subshell
- Kindles modules, sources dependencies, sets configuration
- State visible to all cases within this fixture, dies at fixture boundary
- Registered as third argument to `butr_fixture_enroll`

### Allowed Operations by Layer

| Operation | Parent | Fixture | `_tcase` |
|-----------|--------|---------|----------|
| `z«prefix»_kindle` | — | ✅ | — |
| `source` module files | — | ✅ | — |
| Baste configuration | — | ✅ | — |
| Case iteration | — | ✅ | — |
| Assertions (`buto_*_expect_*`) | — | — | ✅ |
| `zbuto_invoke` (capture) | — | — | ✅ |
| Tabtarget invocation | — | — | ✅ |
| Status tracking (pass/fail/skip) | ✅ | — | — |

### Communication Across Boundaries

| Boundary | Crosses | Does Not Cross |
|----------|---------|----------------|
| Fixture → parent | Exit status (0=pass, non-zero=fail, 2=skip), stdout/stderr | Variables, kindle state, sourced functions |
| `_tcase` → fixture | Exit status (0=pass, non-zero=fail), stdout/stderr | Variable mutations, BURV roots, temp dirs |

### Naming Conventions

| Element | Pattern | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Testbench file | `«prefix»tb_testbench.sh` | `rbtb_testbench.sh` |
| Test case file | `«prefix»tc«xx»_«Name».sh` | `rbtckk_KickTires.sh` |
| Baste function | `z«tb»_«name»_baste` | `zrbtb_ark_baste` |
| Case function | `«tc»_«name»_tcase` | `rbtckk_false_tcase` |
| Fixture enrollment | `butr_fixture_enroll` | — |
| Suite enrollment | `butr_suite_enroll` | Sets suite context |
| Case enrollment | `butr_case_enroll` | — |

### Compliance Checking

The naming conventions enable grep-based auditing:

```bash
# Find all baste functions
grep -rn '_baste' Tools/

# Find all case functions
grep -rn '_tcase' Tools/

# Verify enrollment matches naming — baste/case functions should
# appear both in enrollment calls and as function definitions
grep -rn 'butr_fixture_enroll\|butr_case_enroll' Tools/
```

---

## Shellcheck Integration

BCG uses [shellcheck](https://www.shellcheck.net) for static analysis of bash source with a curated suppress list that eliminates false positives caused by BCG's module architecture.

### Architecture

| Artifact | Location | Purpose |
|----------|----------|---------|
| `busc_shellcheckrc` | `Tools/buk/` | BCG-structural suppressions with rationale |
| `buq_cli.sh` | `Tools/buk/` | BUK qualification CLI hosting shellcheck command |
| `buw-qsc` | Workbench route | Invokes `buq_shellcheck` |
| `buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh` | `tt/` | Tabtarget for shellcheck qualification |

### Suppressed Codes (BCG-structural)

These codes are false positives caused by BCG's dynamic sourcing, cross-module constants, indirect dispatch, and template-generation patterns:

| Code | Reason |
|------|--------|
| SC1090 | Can't follow non-constant source — BCG furnish pattern |
| SC1091 | Not following sourced file — same root cause |
| SC2034 | Variable appears unused — cross-module kindle/tinder constants |
| SC2154 | Variable not assigned — inverse of SC2034 |
| SC2155 | Declare and assign separately — BCG blesses single-line `$(<file)` |
| SC2153 | Possible misspelling — cross-module prefix similarity |
| SC2329 | Function never invoked — indirect invocation via source/dispatch |
| SC2016 | Expressions in single quotes — template-generation patterns echo single-quoted strings containing `${}` into generated files (tabtargets, launchers, Dockerfiles) |
| SC2254 | Unquoted case pattern — BCG glob-matching patterns intentionally use unquoted variables in case statements |
| SC2059 | Variable in printf format string — BCG table-formatting patterns use `local -r` format constants shared across header, separator, and data rows |

### Policy

1. **No inline directives.** All structural suppressions live in `busc_shellcheckrc`. See **Prohibited Constructs § Inline Shellcheck Directives**.
2. **Genuine findings must be fixed.** If shellcheck flags code that isn't BCG-structural, the code has a real issue.
3. **New structural codes** discovered during adoption should be added to `busc_shellcheckrc` with rationale — never suppressed inline.

---

## Fading Memory — Superseded Conventions

This section documents conventions that have been replaced. When encountering legacy code using these patterns, transform to the current convention. Do not bulk-rename across the codebase — migrate opportunistically when touching affected files.

### FM-001: _register → _enroll

**Superseded pattern:**
- Function suffix: `_register`
- Return variables: `z1z_«prefix»_«term»`
- Array naming: no `_roll` suffix convention
- Accessor naming: ad-hoc `_get_*` functions

**Recognition — legacy code looks like:**
- Functions named `*_register` that populate parallel arrays
- Variables starting with `z1z_` used as return channels
- Parallel arrays without `_roll` suffix (e.g., `zbuz_colophons` instead of `z_buz_colophon_roll`)
- Accessor functions named `*_get_*` without `_recite` suffix

**Current convention:**
- Function suffix: `_enroll` (kindle-only)
- Return variables: `z_«funcname»_«retval»` (function name embedded verbatim)
- Array naming: `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll`
- Accessor suffix: `_recite` (read-only, never mutates)

**Known legacy sites:**
- `buz_register` in `Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh`
- `butr_register` in `Tools/buk/butr_registry.sh`

**Migration:** When touching a file that uses the old pattern, transform to new. Enroll calls must move into kindle. Return variables must use `z_«funcname»_«retval»`. Arrays must gain `_roll` suffix using `z_«prefix»_` convention. Accessors must gain `_recite` suffix.

aiTitle: Clarify array storage for zipper facts and code generation

content.text:
Yes — exactly one new roll, and it's narrower than "store the zipper facts." Here's the precise picture, now that I've read BCG end to end.

**The facts are already stored — except the one the emitter needs for the const *name*.** `buz_enroll` (`buz_zipper.sh:106`) takes six args and appends five of them to five parallel rolls:

```
z_buz_colophon_roll   ← the colophon string  "rbw-cC"   (the const VALUE)
z_buz_module_roll
z_buz_command_roll
z_buz_channel_roll
z_buz_describe_roll
```

The sixth arg, `z_varname` (e.g. `RBW_CC`), is used only for the `printf -v` into the caller at line 141 and then **discarded** — it's never rolled. That varname is precisely the symbol you'd want the Rust const named after. So the const *value* is already retained; the const *name* is the missing fact.

**So the change is: add `z_buz_varname_roll` and append `${z_varname}` to it in `buz_enroll`.** That's one more parallel roll, and BCG blesses it unreservedly:
- It's textbook **mutable kindle state** — a `_roll`, initialized empty in `zbuz_kindle`, populated by enroll, immutable after kindle, lowercase, no `readonly` (BCG §"Mutable Kindle State", and `z_buz_colophon_roll` is literally BCG's canonical example of the convention).
- It's **additive**: `printf -v` stays, the five existing rolls stay, every existing `buz_enroll` caller is byte-for-byte unaffected — they already pass the varname.

Then `buz_emit_rust_consts` is a regular function that walks the rolls (index iteration — `for z_i in "${!z_buz_varname_roll[@]}"`, the `set -u`-safe form BCG mandates) and `printf`s `pub const NAME: &str = "...";` to stdout — structurally the same shape as `buz_emit_context`. For the colophon case, name = `z_buz_varname_roll[i]`, value = `z_buz_colophon_roll[i]`.

One BCG note in passing: FM-001 flags `buz_register` as a legacy site, but this file is already on the modern `buz_enroll` / `z_buz_*_roll` naming, so no migration is entangled here.

The only genuinely open design decision is the one I raised before — how the *generic* emitter receives its pairs (named-array args vs stdin vs variadic), since the docket wants RBCC/fixture/scattered projectors to reuse it rather than each reaching into `z_buz_*_roll` by name. That's separable from the roll addition, which is settled. Want me to proceed with the roll first and then we settle the emitter's input shape, or pin the input shape now?

message.content: Chuckle, I'm not quite understanding you when you say 'generic emitter': is this a question of affiliating zipper entry declaration with the right rust prefix to use?  I'm just not getting you yet

content.text:
No — it's not about prefixes at all. The const *name* for a colophon is just the enroll varname verbatim (`RBW_CC`); there's nothing to choose. Let me re-ground, because "generic" is doing all the work in that sentence and I under-explained it.

The word is about **one fork: is the emitter function colophon-specific, or a reusable pairs-to-consts printer?**

**Reading A — colophon-specific.** `buz_emit_rust_consts` reaches *inside itself* for the zipper rolls — it loops `z_buz_varname_roll` + `z_buz_colophon_roll` and prints the colophon consts. It only ever does colophons. When the later paces in this heat need to project the RBCC set, the fixture names, the operation verbs — each gets its *own* emitter function. Bespoke codegen per group.

**Reading B — generic (what the docket locked).** `buz_emit_rust_consts` knows nothing about colophons. It takes a list of `(name, value)` and prints:

```rust
pub const NAME: &str = "value";
```

...for each. The *colophon caller* is what walks the rolls and hands the pairs in (`RBW_CC` → `rbw-cC`, etc.). Later, the RBCC caller hands in its own pairs; the fixture caller hands in its own. **One printer, many feeders.**

Why this matters *for this pace specifically*: this is the heat's **first** projector, and the paddock's locked decision is "one BUK mechanism every projector reuses." So if we build Reading A here, we've quietly prejudged the mechanism the rest of the heat is supposed to share — and the next pace re-invents codegen. Building Reading B means RBCC/fixtures/verbs paces are just "assemble my pairs, call the printer."

So the rolls work we settled (retaining the varname) feeds the colophon caller; "generic" is about keeping that caller *outside* the printer so the printer is reusable. The genuinely-open bit is purely plumbing: how does a bash caller hand "a list of pairs" to a function — two named arrays, lines on stdin, or flat `name value name value` args. That's the only thing left to decide; everything above it is the docket's settled shape.

Does that fork land now?

message.content: We are definitely bash 3.2, so limited array and map support...  I haven't thought this through, I don't yet have an opinion on what is more maintainable... but maybe keeping zipper ideas (and we have 2: buk, rbk) integral and adding a separate mechanism for the other constants is better.  New BCG module with _enroll nature like zipper, rhyming?

content.text:
Your instinct is tracking the bash 3.2 reality better than the docket's wording does. Here's why it lands:

**There is no clean way to hand a bash 3.2 function "a list of pairs."** No associative arrays, and passing an array *by name* forces `eval` (or the awkward `${!name[@]}` that doesn't do what you'd hope). So the docket's phrase "a generic emitter that takes name/value pairs" has an unstated plumbing problem the moment you try to write the signature. The *native* bash accumulation primitive for name/value pairs is exactly the thing the zipper already is: **a roll, fed by `_enroll`.** So "generic mechanism that takes pairs" is most faithfully realized as *a second enroll registry*, not a function with array arguments.

That reframing matters: **your proposal doesn't break the paddock's locked "one mechanism" decision — it's the bash-native realization of it.** "Name/value pairs" becomes "enroll calls into a constants roll." Same intent, a shape that fits the language.

And there's a concrete reason the new constants *can't* just ride `buz_enroll`: the zipper's enroll is a **6-tuple** (varname, colophon, module, command, channel, description) — colophon-shaped. RBCC constants, fixture names, verbs, container roles are simple **2-tuples** (name, value). Different shape → a simpler sibling enroll is the right fit, not a contortion of the existing one. It rhymes structurally: kindle inits the roll, `_enroll NAME VALUE` appends, `_emit` walks and prints `pub const NAME: &str = "VALUE";`.

The real payoff of the enroll shape — and why it beats a function-taking-pairs — is **declare-and-register in one call.** Today the zipper's `buz_enroll` does double duty: `printf -v` sets the caller's variable *and* registers it. A sibling could do the same for plain constants: the single canonical home (RBCC, rbte, …) calls `_enroll FOO bar`, which both *is* the bash declaration and registers it for projection. That's what actually kills the dual-maintenance — there's no second list to drift.

So the shape I think you're reaching for is two modules sharing one wire format:
- **zipper** keeps its colophon consts integral (it already holds the rolls) — both buk and rbk instances;
- **a sibling constants-enroll module** is the home for everything else;
- both print through one tiny shared per-pair primitive (`printf 'pub const %s: &str = "%s";\n'`) so the Rust syntax is single-sourced and the two emit sites can't drift.

That last point is the one genuine sub-decision: **does the zipper *feed* the sibling registry (truly one emit site, purest "one mechanism"), or stay self-contained and merely *share the format primitive* (two emit sites, zipper independent)?** Feeding is more unified; staying integral avoids re-enrolling data the zipper already holds. I lean toward *share-the-primitive, stay integral* — the zipper's data is already roll-shaped and self-owned, so routing it through a second registry buys uniformity at the cost of redundant enrollment.

(Prefix for the new module is a later mint — `bu?` family, RBK-ignorant per the docket — not worth picking until the shape settles.)

One flag before we go further: this revises *how* the paddock-locked "generic / one mechanism" decision gets realized — not its intent, but its surface (a new module instead of a function, and possibly two emit sites). That's a paddock/docket-shape change, so once you've got an opinion I'd want to recurry the paddock and reslate ₢BPAAC rather than let the docket and the code drift apart. No code yet — does the share-the-primitive / stay-integral split feel right, or do you want the zipper feeding the registry too?

Maybe one capture function per array- do note they are co-indexed, i.e. guaranteed by design to have same number and one-to-one and onto mapping.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Good — and the co-indexing point you're emphasizing is exactly the right thing to lean on. Let me reflect it back and then offer one simplification.

You're right that the two rolls are co-indexed by design: same length, 1:1, onto. That guarantee is *already* what `buz_emit_context` relies on — it walks the colophon roll by index (`for i in "${!...colophon_roll[@]}"`) and reads the parallel rolls at the same `i`. So a single index walk gives you both the varname and the colophon in lockstep. You've independently re-derived why the existing pattern works.

Here's the simplification: **if the generation loop lives inside buz, you don't need an accessor at all.** Two ways to use that co-indexing:

- **Loop inside buz** — write a twin of `buz_emit_context` that does the same index walk but prints `pub const RBW_CC: &str = "rbw-cC";` instead of a markdown row. It reads its own rolls directly. No accessor, no transport, no re-zipping. One new function, modeled line-for-line on one that already exists.

- **Loop outside buz** — buz exposes accessors, some external generator reads them and does the formatting. Now you need: write the accessors, get two lists safely across the `$()` boundary, then re-zip them by index in the caller. Three steps instead of one.

The accessor route only earns its keep if something *other than const-emission* needs to read those rolls from outside buz — and right now nothing does. So for "keep it in buz and simple," the internal twin is actually *less* code than the accessor.

(One BCG nit, since it'll matter when you write it: read-only roll access is a `_recite` function, not `_capture`. `_capture` is for computed/secret values; `_recite` is the blessed name for "hand back what's in a roll." But per the above, the internal twin needs neither.)

The thing this quietly does: it scopes *this* pace down to "the zipper projects its own colophon consts," and leaves RBCC / fixtures / verbs / container-roles to their own later paces. That's a reasonable call — but it does step away from the paddock's "one generic mechanism every projector reuses" decision, so once you're settled I'd recurry the paddock to match. No code yet.

Does the internal-twin-of-`buz_emit_context` picture feel like the simple thing you were reaching for?

content.text:
Yes — that pathway confirms your instinct, and it's enough for me to have a clear opinion now.

The "only need the rust when we need it" point is exactly right: `buz_emit_context` is dormant until `rblm_generate` (the `rbw-MG` command, `rblm_cli.sh:388`) calls it to write the markdown context file on demand. The qualifier (`rbq_Qualify.sh:138`) then regenerates fresh and diffs against the committed copy, dying "stale" on drift. That's *already* the build-time diff gate this heat wants — it exists for markdown today.

**My opinion: build the internal twin, scoped to colophons, and defer the generic mechanism.** Concretely, three small moves, each modeled on something already there:

1. **buz** gains `z_buz_varname_roll` — one line in `zbuz_kindle`, one append in `buz_enroll`. (The varname-retention we settled.)
2. **buz** gains `buz_emit_rust_consts` — a twin of `buz_emit_context`: same index walk over the co-indexed rolls, but it prints `pub const RBW_CC: &str = "rbw-cC";` instead of a markdown row. Reads its own rolls; no accessor, no module, no pair-passing.
3. **rblm_generate** calls it alongside the markdown emit, writing the Rust file; **rbq_Qualify** gets the same fresh-generate-and-diff gate the markdown file already has.

Why this is the simplest *and* most maintainable:

- **Smallest diff, proven pattern.** A maintainer who reads `buz_emit_context` understands its Rust twin instantly — they're the same loop. That's the lowest cognitive cost any option here offers.
- **The generic mechanism is speculative generality, and bash 3.2 punishes it.** The "pass name/value pairs to a function" form has no clean signature in 3.2. Building it now, against a *single* example (colophons), is guessing at the abstraction. When RBCC actually needs projecting, you'll have a *second concrete* emit site — and that's the honest moment to factor out a shared `pub const` format primitive, because you'll see what the two truly share. Two examples, then abstract; not one example and a prophecy.
- **Zero new naming, zero new surface.** No `bu?` prefix to mint, no second module to document.

The cost I'll name plainly: this walks away from the paddock's locked "one generic mechanism every projector reuses" decision. I think that decision was made before the bash 3.2 plumbing reality was weighed, and you're the right person to revise it. So my recommendation is to **scope ₢BPAAC to "the zipper projects its colophon consts"** and let each later constant group get its own pace — factoring a shared primitive out only once a second emit site exists.

If that sits right, the next step is to recurry the paddock and reslate ₢BPAAC to match this shape — *then* write code. Want me to draft those docket/paddock edits for your review?

input.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BP

## Shape

Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.

The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, fixture names, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.

## Locked decisions

- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
- **No pre-built generic emitter; each owner emits its own consts.** bash 3.2 has no clean way to hand a pair-list to a function (no associative arrays, array-by-name forces eval), so the native realization is: each constant group's canonical bash home walks its own data and prints `pub const` lines, modeled on the existing `buz_emit_context` markdown emitter. A shared `pub const` format primitive is factored out only once a second emit site exists — two concrete cases, then abstract, not one example and a prophecy. The zipper projects its colophons integrally; later groups each earn their own projector pace.
- **Full co-maintained set is in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc), fixture names (rbte). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earn a canonical-author tidy first (give them one bash home), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.

## What done looks like

The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The rbtd manifest's hand-mirrored groups and the lib.rs path macros are fed from generation; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).

input.content:
# jjezs_reslate ₢BPAAC

## Character

Mechanical, additive, low-risk. The one design choice is settled: the emit lives in the zipper as a twin of `buz_emit_context`, scoped to colophons. RBK-ignorant — the zipper names nothing domain-specific.

## Goal

Make the zipper project its enrolled colophons to Rust consts on demand, mirroring how `buz_emit_context` projects them to markdown. Colophons only — other constant groups are later paces.

## Shape

- `buz_enroll` must retain the enroll varname in a new co-indexed roll. Today the varname is `printf -v`'d into the caller and discarded; it is the symbol the Rust const is named after. Additive: `printf -v` and the existing rolls stay, existing callers untouched.
- Add a sibling emit function modeled line-for-line on `buz_emit_context`: same index walk over the co-indexed rolls, printing `pub const VARNAME: &str = "colophon";` to stdout. It reads its own rolls — no accessor, no generic pair-passing. Varname roll supplies the const name, colophon roll the value.
- Wire the generation command to emit the Rust file alongside the markdown, and give that file the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness gate the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context.

## Sources

- `Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh` — `buz_enroll` (varname discard), `buz_emit_context` (emit template), `zbuz_kindle` (roll init)
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rblm_generate` (the markdown emit site to extend)
- `Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh` — the fresh-vs-committed staleness gate to mirror

## What done looks like

A varname roll exists and the new emit walks the co-indexed rolls to produce `pub const` lines for every enrolled colophon. The Rust file is generated by the generation command and gated stale-on-drift by the qualifier, the same as the markdown. Acceptance is the emitted output matching the enrolled colophons; the BUK self-test is a no-regression check, not emitter verification.

file.content:
local z_cred_name=""
    local z_any_copied=0
    for z_cred in "${z_source_secrets}"/*.env; do
      test -f "${z_cred}" || continue
      z_cred_name="${z_cred##*/}"
      cp "${z_cred}" "${z_target_secrets}/${z_cred_name}" || buc_die "Failed to copy credential: ${z_cred_name}"
      chmod 600 "${z_target_secrets}/${z_cred_name}" || buc_die "Failed to set permissions on: ${z_cred_name}"
      buh_line "  Copied: secrets/${z_cred_name}"
      z_any_copied=1
    done
    test "${z_any_copied}" = "1" || buh_line "  No credential files found in: ${z_source_secrets}"
  else
    buh_line "  Warning: source secrets directory not found: ${z_source_secrets}"
  fi

  buh_e
  buh_line "  Proof complete: ${z_clone_dir}"
  buh_e
  buh_line "  To use the duplicate, start Claude Code from:"
  buh_line "    ${z_clone_dir}"
  buc_success "Proof copy created at ${z_target_dir}"
}

######################################################################
# Command: generate - Generate tabtarget context markdown from zipper registry

rblm_generate() {
  buc_doc_brief "Generate tabtarget context markdown from zipper registry"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  local -r z_target="${RBCC_KIT_DIR}/rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md"
  local -r z_tmp="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md"

  buz_emit_context "${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}" > "${z_tmp}" || buc_die "Failed to generate context"

  if test -f "${z_target}" && diff -q "${z_tmp}" "${z_target}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    buc_log_args "Context file already up to date: ${z_target}"
  else
    cp "${z_tmp}" "${z_target}" || buc_die "Failed to write: ${z_target}"
    buc_log_args "Generated: ${z_target}"
  fi

  buc_success "Context generation complete"
}

######################################################################
# Furnish and Main

zrblm_furnish() {
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TEMP_DIR         " "Temporary directory for this invocation (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TABTARGET_DIR    " "Tabtarget directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  local z_rbk_kit_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbcc_Constants.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source rbcc_Constants.sh"

  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buym_yelp.sh"         || buc_die "Failed to source buym_yelp.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buh_handbook.sh"      || buc_die "Failed to source buh_handbook.sh"

aiTitle: Clarify array storage for zipper facts and code generation

content.text:
Stopping to surface a discovery before writing code — it affects what "done" means for this pace, and scope segmentation is your call.

**The bash varnames and the Rust const names don't match, and the Rust side is a subset.**

| | bash enroll varname | Rust const (today) |
|---|---|---|
| charge | `RBZ_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE` | `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_CHARGE` |
| writ | `RBZ_CRUCIBLE_WRIT` | `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_WRIT` |

Two gaps: the **prefix** differs (`RBZ_` vs `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_`), the **stem** differs (`CRUCIBLE_CHARGE` vs `CHARGE`), and `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` hand-declares only the **~30 colophons theurge actually invokes** (plus credential roles), while the zipper holds all **~80**.

This matters because the zipper is RBK-ignorant — the *only* name it has for each entry is the enroll varname. So a faithful `buz_emit_rust_consts` can only emit `pub const RBZ_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE: &str = "rbw-cC";`. It **cannot** produce `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_CHARGE` — that name is RBK knowledge the zipper doesn't (and shouldn't) have. So this pace's emitter cannot directly *replace* `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`; reconciling the two naming schemes (rename the rbtd consumers to `RBZ_*`, or make `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*` thin aliases of the generated consts) and retiring the runtime drift check is necessarily a separate, RBK-aware step.

**My recommended boundary for ₢BPAAC** (matches the reslated "colophons only / acceptance = emitted output matches enrolled colophons"):

1. Add the varname roll + `buz_emit_rust_consts` (emits `RBZ_*` verbatim for **all** enrolled colophons).
2. Wire `rblm_generate` to write a **new generated `.rs`** into the rbtd crate (e.g. a `*_generated.rs`), with the same fresh-vs-committed staleness gate.
3. **Leave `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` and its consumers untouched** — the reconciliation + drift-check retirement becomes the next pace (I'll cantle it).

That keeps this pace small, mechanical, and verifiable on its own (the generated file's consts match the registry), and doesn't entangle it with the rbtd rename.

Proceed on that boundary? The alternative — fold the rbtdrm rename and drift-check retirement into *this* pace — is bigger and I'd advise against it, but it's your scope call.

message.content: Hold on, make sure you understand my minting desires when it comes to naming.  I think you are pointing out that having a wholly generated file is way easier than editing an existing file, and I thoroughly agree.  Did I understand?

content.text:
Yes — you understood my point exactly. A wholly generated file (that nothing hand-edits) is far easier and safer than surgically rewriting `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`: no merge with hand-authored content, no risk of clobbering the credential-role section or the `rbtdrm_credential_check_colophon` fn, and the staleness gate just compares the whole file byte-for-byte.

Now let me say back what I think your **minting** desire is, so you can correct me — because this is the part I want to be sure of:

The freedom of a wholly generated file is that **its symbol names are dictated by the generator, not inherited from legacy.** Since the zipper is RBK-ignorant, the only name it owns per entry is the enroll varname — so the generated consts are the bash varname **verbatim**: `pub const RBZ_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE: &str = "rbw-cC";`. That makes the enroll varname *the* cross-language symbol — one identifier, one canonical spelling, living in both bash and Rust. No new prefix is minted (it's already `RBZ_`, the zipper's terminal namespace), and the legacy `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*` names carry **no obligation to be preserved** — they're redundant the moment the generated file exists, and get retired or reduced to thin aliases in the follow-up pace.

So the mint discipline here is: *don't invent a name for the projection; the projection carries the source-of-truth name across the boundary unchanged.*

Where I'm genuinely unsure of your desire — two open points:

1. **The generated file's own name.** It lives in the rbtd crate but holds `RBZ_*` symbols, not `rbtd*` symbols. Convention says a file's prefix matches its symbols, which argues the filename should be `rbz`-flavored even though it sits under `rbtd/src/`. Do you want it named for its *contents* (`rbz…`) or its *home* (`rbtd…`)?

2. **Whether the consts stay `RBZ_` in Rust or get re-homed.** Verbatim `RBZ_` is the zero-mint answer. But if you'd rather the Rust side carry an rbtd-family name, that's a *transform* the generator would apply — and a transform reintroduces exactly the drift we're killing. I lean hard toward verbatim, but it's your namespace.

Did I get your philosophy right, and which way on those two?

message.content: RBZ_ is _not_ good minting for our rust variables, since that is a bash only identifier.  This should be a prefix relative to the new module as per BCG: we should be making a full module with unique minting (though I doubt we need a _cli.sh file as part of it).  Thoughts?  Hmm, remind me of rust minting, I think; is there a way to say 'constants, generated'?

content.text:
Good correction — and you're right on the principle. `RBZ_` in Rust isn't a *syntax* problem (SCREAMING_SNAKE is legal in both languages); it's a *minting* problem: `RBZ_` denotes "the rbz zipper bash module owns this symbol," and stamping that ownership onto a Rust const in the rbtd crate is a false claim. The semantic owner of the generated Rust consts is the new module, so the prefix must be that module's. Verbatim-across-languages only works if the *single canonical name* is minted to be valid and meaningful in both universes — which `RBZ_` is not.

Let me pull the actual Rust minting conventions rather than wing it.

content.content:
13:The primary concern is **minting discipline** — ensuring all identifiers follow the project's prefix naming rules for clarity, grep-ability, and ease of discussion in non-Rust-aware contexts.
25:- `non_camel_case_types`: Allows prefixed type names like `jjrg_Gallops`
26:- `private_interfaces`: Allows z-prefixed (internal) types in public enum variants
29:The `allow` directives must appear after `deny(warnings)` to override it for RCG-specific naming patterns. The `deny` directive ensures warnings never accumulate — a warning today becomes a mystery tomorrow.
72:## File Naming
76:Pattern: `{cipher}r{classifier}_{name}.rs`
82:| `{classifier}` | Single letter, name typically starts with this letter |
86:- `jjrg_gallops.rs` — JJ Rust, classifier `g`, "gallops"
87:- `jjrc_core.rs` — JJ Rust, classifier `c`, "core"
88:- `vvcg_guard.rs` — VV Core, classifier `g`, "guard"
92:Pattern: `{cipher}t{classifier}_{name}.rs`
94:Tests use `t` instead of `r` after the cipher, with matching classifier:
102:Module wiring in `lib.rs`:
110:## Declaration Naming
112:**All public declarations from a source file must start with that file's full prefix.**
116:### Naming Patterns by Type
118:For a file with prefix `jjrg`:
122:| Struct | `{prefix}_PascalName` | `jjrg_Gallops` | `z{prefix}_PascalName` | `zjjrg_Output` |
123:| Enum | `{prefix}_PascalName` | `jjrg_PaceState` | `z{prefix}_PascalName` | `zjjrg_ParseMode` |
124:| Function | `{prefix}_snake_name` | `jjrg_load()` | `z{prefix}_snake_name` | `zjjrg_validate()` |
125:| Constant | `{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` | `JJRG_DEFAULT_PATH` | `Z{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` | `ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH` |
126:| Trait | `{prefix}_TraitName` | `jjrg_Storable` | `z{prefix}_TraitName` | `zjjrg_Parseable` |
127:| Type alias | `{prefix}_AliasName` | `jjrg_Result` | `z{prefix}_AliasName` | `zjjrg_InternalResult` |
131:Methods on prefixed types also carry the prefix:
144:**The `z`/`Z` prefix applies uniformly to all internal declaration types** — structs, enums, functions, constants, traits, and type aliases. The prefix letter follows the casing context: lowercase `z` for snake_case and PascalCase names, uppercase `Z` for SCREAMING_SNAKE constants. See the Internal Pattern columns in the naming table above.
146:This parallels BCG's `z«prefix»_` convention for bash. The key difference: BCG also uses `z_«name»` for local variables because bash has flat function scope. Rust's block scoping eliminates this need — local variables use normal names without any z-prefix.
155:pub(crate) const ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH: usize = 16;
162:The `z`/`Z` prefix signals "do not call from outside this module's conceptual boundary" even though Rust visibility permits crate-internal access.
166:When using derive macros like `clap`, internal argument structs (`z{prefix}_*Args`) may appear in public enum variants. Rust warns about this visibility mismatch. The `#![allow(private_interfaces)]` attribute in Crate Boilerplate suppresses this expected noise — the `z` prefix communicates intent to humans even when Rust forces technical exposure.
170:When tests construct internal structs directly, both the struct AND its fields need `pub(crate)` visibility:
173:// ❌ Tests can't construct this — fields are private
179:// ✅ Tests can construct this — fields are accessible
186:If tests only READ internal structs (returned by functions), field visibility doesn't matter. But if tests CONSTRUCT them for unit testing serialization, validation, or helper logic, fields must be `pub(crate)`.
190:Test functions in `jjt{classifier}_{name}.rs` files use the test file's prefix:
210:| Level | Visibility | Prefix | Testable |
212:| Public API | `pub` | `{prefix}_` | Yes |
213:| Internal | `pub(crate)` | `z{prefix}_` | Yes |
216:Prefer `pub(crate)` with `z` prefix over true private for anything that benefits from testing. Reserve true private for trivial implementation details.
218:## Minting Discipline
220:All naming follows the project's minting rules. Key constraints:
222:1. **Terminal exclusivity**: A prefix either IS a name or HAS children, never both
224:3. **Grep-ability**: `grep jjrg_` finds all declarations from that module
226:Reference: CLAUDE.md "Prefix Naming Discipline" section.
239:2. **No tolerances**: Do not add `strip_prefix`, case folding, alias acceptance, or alternative parse paths unless the specification explicitly requires temporary backwards compatibility.
242:5. **Documentation uses canonical form**: All documentation, examples, and generated content must use the single canonical form — never a tolerated alternative, even if the implementation happens to accept it.
246:- **String Boundary Discipline** is the serialization-specific instance: one const, exact match, no friendly parsing
247:- **Minting Discipline** is the naming-specific instance: one canonical name per terminal prefix
260:Any string value that appears in serialization or display must have exactly **one** const definition. Both parse and emit reference that const.
263:// ✅ Single definition - const is the source of truth
264:const JJRG_STATE_ROUGH: &str = "rough";
315:If input doesn't match the canonical const exactly, that's a bug in the producer — not a variant to handle gracefully.
323:When manual methods are needed (e.g., for display or CLI), define consts that match the serde representation.
327:If `grep '"rough"'` finds the same string literal in multiple places, the interface is immature. Extract a const.
329:## Constant Discipline (Beyond Strings)
335:Any magic value that appears 2+ times requires a const. This includes numbers, format strings, and computed values.
339:If value B depends on value A, always derive B from A at the use site. Never define B as a separate constant.
342:// ✅ Length derived from the const
343:pub const JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT: &str = "0000000";
351:const COMMIT_LEN: usize = 7;  // Could diverge from JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT
357:The const name must identify the specific value, not just its category. A category-only suffix is ambiguous and becomes a terminal exclusivity violation the moment a second value appears in the same category.

content.text:
Pulled the conventions. Here's the reminder, then my thoughts on the module.

**Rust minting (from RCG):**
- A file is `{cipher}{r|t}{classifier}_{name}.rs` — cipher is the crate/kit, `r`=runtime / `t`=test, classifier is a single letter (the name usually starts with it). E.g. `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` = cipher `rbtd`, runtime, classifier `m`.
- **Constants take the file's full prefix, uppercased**: file `rbtdrm` → `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_CHARGE`. The const prefix is *inseparable from the module* — which is exactly the thing `RBZ_` violates (its module is the bash zipper, not this Rust file).

So to your question — **there is no "generated" sigil in RCG.** Generated-ness isn't carried in the prefix; it's carried two other ways:
1. A header doc-comment banner (the way `buz_emit_context` writes `<!-- Generated... Do not edit -->`), and
2. RCG's *canonical-form* rule — "generated content must use the single canonical form." A generated file is just a normal module that happens to be machine-written; its naming is ordinary.

So you don't mint a prefix meaning "generated constants." You mint a prefix meaning **what the constants *are*** (colophons / the cross-language colophon set) and *mark* the file generated in its header. If you want the classifier to hint it, `c` for "colophons" is the natural letter — but plain `c` is already taken in rbtd-runtime (`rbtdrc_crucible.rs`), so the classifier choice depends on which crate this module lives in.

**On the new module itself:** yes — a BCG **library module with no `_cli.sh` is explicitly blessed** ("CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution… omit for library/utility modules"). So a constants-owning module with kindle + tinder/enroll + an emit function, no CLI, is in-pattern.

But naming the prefix forces the architectural fork I think you're actually circling, and I don't want to pick it for you:

- **Re-home (no transform).** The new module becomes the *single canonical home* of the colophon set (which is literally the paddock's "single canonical bash author" decision). It defines each colophon once under its own minted prefix — say `XXX_CHARGE = "rbw-cC"` — used by bash *and* emitted to Rust verbatim under the same name. One identifier, both languages, zero mapping. Clean, and it's the honest end-state — but it's bigger than "buz twin": the zipper's `buz_enroll` calls would reference the new module's constants instead of carrying the colophon strings inline, and `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` collapses into the generated file.

- **Transform-at-emit.** Keep `buz`'s rolls as-is and have the generator rename `RBZ_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE` → `XXX_CHARGE` while emitting. Smaller, but it *reintroduces a name mapping* — the exact drift surface this heat exists to kill. I'd advise against it.

I lean hard toward **re-home**, because it's the only one that yields a true single-source name and it satisfies the paddock's canonical-author decision directly. The cost is this pace grows from "teach the zipper to emit" into "stand up the canonical colophon module and point the zipper at it."

Two things I need from you before I mint anything: (1) re-home or transform? and (2) does this new module live *inside the rbtd crate* (consts consumed there) or stand as its own crate/module that rbtd depends on? Once you say, I'll check the tree for a clean prefix + classifier and propose the actual name.

Disk survey (threshold 85%): 228 GB: 60%, 228 GB: 60%
Heat: rbk-13-mvp-redundancy-cleanup (₣BP)
Status: racing
Created: 260520


## Paces

### buz-rust-const-emitter (₢BPAAC) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical, additive, low-risk. The one design choice is settled: the emit lives in the zipper as a twin of `buz_emit_context`, scoped to colophons. RBK-ignorant — the zipper names nothing domain-specific.

## Goal

Make the zipper project its enrolled colophons to Rust consts on demand, mirroring how `buz_emit_context` projects them to markdown. Colophons only — other constant groups are later paces.

## Shape

- `buz_enroll` must retain the enroll varname in a new co-indexed roll. Today the varname is `printf -v`'d into the caller and discarded; it is the symbol the Rust const is named after. Additive: `printf -v` and the existing rolls stay, existing callers untouched.
- Add a sibling emit function modeled line-for-line on `buz_emit_context`: same index walk over the co-indexed rolls, printing `pub const VARNAME: &str = "colophon";` to stdout. It reads its own rolls — no accessor, no generic pair-passing. Varname roll supplies the const name, colophon roll the value.
- Wire the generation command to emit the Rust file alongside the markdown, and give that file the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness gate the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context.

## Sources

- `Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh` — `buz_enroll` (varname discard), `buz_emit_context` (emit template), `zbuz_kindle` (roll init)
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rblm_generate` (the markdown emit site to extend)
- `Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh` — the fresh-vs-committed staleness gate to mirror

## What done looks like

A varname roll exists and the new emit walks the co-indexed rolls to produce `pub const` lines for every enrolled colophon. The Rust file is generated by the generation command and gated stale-on-drift by the qualifier, the same as the markdown. Acceptance is the emitted output matching the enrolled colophons; the BUK self-test is a no-regression check, not emitter verification.

### colophon-codegen-projection (₢BPAAD) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical once the emitter exists, but broad — a consumer rename across the rbtd Rust sources.

## Goal

Generate the rbtd colophon consts from the rbz registry via the BUK emitter and migrate every consumer to the generated names, retiring the hand-mirror. The runtime drift check stays alive here and is retired only when the diff-gate replaces it.

## Shape

- Wire the rbz colophon rolls through the BUK emitter to a checked-in generated Rust file.
- Generated const names carry the enroll varname verbatim — this retires the curated `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*` names, a rename across their reference sites.
- Migrate the Rust consumers off `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*` and the inline colophon literals (e.g. the fast suite) onto the generated consts. Discover sites with `grep -rln "RBTDRM_COLOPHON" Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/`.
- Repoint `rbtdrm_required_colophons` to the generated names but keep `rbtdrm_verify` running. Do NOT retire verify here — that belongs with the diff-gate landing, so drift protection is continuous (no gap between this pace and the gate).
- Out of scope, left hand-written: `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*`, `RBTDRM_OPERATION_*`, `RBTDRM_CONTAINER_*` — no single canonical bash author.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh` — colophon registry
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — hand-mirror + `rbtdrm_verify` + `rbtdrm_required_colophons`

## What done looks like

- Colophon consts are generated, not hand-written; consumers and inline literals reference the generated names; `rbtdrm_verify` still runs (against generated consts); Rust builds and the fast suite is green.

### rbcc-tinder-codegen-projection (₢BPAAE) [rough]

## Character

RBK projector over the single-homed RBCC constants; supersedes the lib.rs path macros, the manifest's credential-role mirror, and the scattered .env-filename literals. Broader than originally scoped — covers the full RBCC-owned co-maintained set.

## Goal

Project every co-maintained constant that `rbcc_Constants.sh` canonically owns to generated Rust consts via the generic BUK emitter, retiring the hand-maintained mirrors.

## Shape

- Scope DECIDED (full single-homed RBCC set): moorings dir, vessels dir, credential roles, and .env filenames. Roles and .env names are hand-copied into Rust today — bringing them under generation removes a live drift risk, it does not create a new home.
- Feeds the generic emitter from the emitter pace as name/value pairs — no standalone RBK codegen.

content.text:
Pulled the whole heat. Six remaining paces, all `[rough]`: the emitter (₢BPAAC), then four group-projections (colophons ₢BPAAD, RBCC set ₢BPAAE, fixtures ₢BPAAG, verbs+roles ₢BPAAH), then build-wiring + drift-gate retirement (₢BPAAF). Two paces already landed (ifrit-retire, scattered-const-bash-home).

First, your direct ask.

## Helping you see my concern — and conceding it

My worry was a syllogism: *Rust name ≠ bash name → some per-entry name correspondence has to be declared and maintained → that's the very drift this heat exists to kill.*

Here's where it breaks, and why you're right: the correspondence **isn't per-entry data — it's a single uniform transform.** Rust const = `<file-prefix>` + `<bash varname with its bash-module prefix stripped>`, **stem carried verbatim**:

```
RBZ_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE  →  strip RBZ_  →  CRUCIBLE_CHARGE  →  RBTDxx_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE
```

One rule for the whole file, fully generated, never hand-touched. No mapping table, no drift.

What fooled me was the *legacy* `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_CHARGE`: whoever hand-wrote it chose a **different stem** (dropped `CRUCIBLE`, added `COLOPHON`), which made it *look* like Rust names require independent authoring. But that arbitrariness was a symptom of hand-maintenance, not a requirement. Generating fresh, we keep the bash stem and swap only the namespace. So your "clean mapping place" is exactly: *one file where the swap has been applied* — and the only correspondence a reader ever needs (same stem, different prefix) is trivial. Less to think about, and it skips re-home's big refactor entirely. **Conceded — your approach wins.**

## But grooming surfaces a reshape — and it makes one of *my own* paddock edits stale

Your "other constant sets are coming in" instinct is the load-bearing point. Look at the heat: **five groups all land in one generated file** (colophons, RBCC set, fixtures, verbs, container roles). That changes the mechanism question:

- A single output file fed by five groups *is* "one shared mechanism" — which swings back toward the heat's **original** paddock decision, and means the revision I made mid-conversation ("no pre-built generic emitter; each owner emits its own") is now the stale thing.
- The bash-3.2 reality still holds, but it's reconciled: there's **one RBK-ignorant per-pair primitive** (`format one pub const line`), and **each group's bash home loops its own data through it** (per-pair scalar calls are trivial in 3.2; it was only *passing a whole pair-list to a function* that was ugly). Generic mechanism + per-owner feeders. Both halves of our earlier argument were half-right.

So `₢BPAAC` (which I reslated toward a buz-only, verbatim-name emitter) and `₢BPAAD` (says "const names carry the enroll varname verbatim") now contradict both your decision and their own siblings `₢BPAAE/G/H` (which already say "generic emitter, name/value pairs"). Ironically my AAC reslate is what knocked them out of alignment.

## The naming impact you flagged

Because the file hosts **all five groups**, its prefix must be **group-agnostic** — named for *being the generated bash-mirror constants for rbtd*, not for colophons. And to your "constants, generated" question: **RCG has no generated sigil.** Generated-ness is carried by (1) a header `// Generated… do not edit` banner and (2) RCG's canonical-form rule. So you name the file for its *content* and *mark* it generated — the prefix is ordinary.

Concretely it's a new `rbtd` runtime file, `rbtdr{classifier}_{name}.rs`, consts `RBTDR?_…`. Taken runtime classifiers in the crate: `m e d c p f o x b i k l g`. Free and plausibly mnemonic: `r` (mirror? taken sense-wise is fine), or a deliberate pick. This is your mint and you have the strong opinions — **what letter/name do you want?** My only constraint-input: it should read as "the mirror/projection of bash constants," e.g. classifier for *mirror* or *reflected*. Once you pick, I'll verify terminal-exclusivity against the tree before committing.

## Proposed groom operations (after you give the prefix)

1. **Re-revise the paddock** emitter decision: one generated landing file + one minted rbtd prefix + shared per-pair RBK-ignorant primitive + the mechanical swap-prefix-keep-stem rule (no per-entry mapping). Supersedes my earlier "no generic emitter" bullet.
2. **Mass-reslate** `₢BPAAC` and `₢BPAAD` off "verbatim enroll varname" onto the minted-prefix + mechanical-stem shape; light touch on `₢BPAAE/G/H` to name the shared file/prefix; `₢BPAAF` stays.

Give me the prefix and I'll execute the paddock re-revision and the mass reslate in one pass.

content.text:
Honest answer: the codebase **doesn't decompose the `d`.** Both the acronym map and `Cargo.toml` gloss `rbtd` only as *"Theurge — crucible test orchestrator for Recipe Bottle"* — no letter-by-letter expansion exists anywhere I can find. (The `rbt*` hits in RBS0 are spec-internal quoin categories — `rbtr_`, `rbtgo_` — unrelated to the crate.)

My **inference**, flagged as inference not authority: `rb` = Recipe Bottle, `rbt` is the test family (the `*t_` = Testbench suffix), and within it `rbtw` = Workbench, `rbtd` = the Theurge orchestrator — with `d` as a bare content letter (plausibly *driver/dispatcher* for an orchestrator, but that's me reading tea leaves). Since we're minting in this tree anyway, documenting the `d` in the acronym map would be a cheap spook-fix while we're here.

**On `r` — it checks out cleanly.** Classifier `r` for *reflection/mirror*. The file becomes `rbtd` + `r` (runtime marker) + `r` (mirror classifier) = **`rbtdrr_*.rs`**, consts **`RBTDRR_*`**:

```rust
pub const RBTDRR_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE: &str = "rbw-cC";
```

I verified the tree: runtime classifiers taken are `m e d c p f o x b i k l g` — **`rbtdrr` is free**, terminal-exclusive. The doubled `r` is just the runtime-marker-`r` meeting the mirror-classifier-`r`; a little funny to the eye but fully in-scheme.

Two small confirms and I'll execute the whole groom (paddock re-revision + mass reslate) in one pass:

1. **The name word.** RCG's soft convention is "name starts with the classifier letter," so an r-word for the mirror sense: `rbtdrr_reflected.rs` is my pick (vs. `mirror`, which starts with `m`). Good, or do you want a different word?
2. **Prefix locked at `RBTDRR_`** for all five projected groups (colophons get `RBTDRR_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE`, etc.)?

Give me those and I'll re-revise the paddock and mass-reslate ₢BPAAC/AAD/AAE/AAG/AAH against the `RBTDRR_` shape.

lastPrompt: Can you remind me what the 'd' in rbtd is?  I do like your 'r' diea.

content.text:
Checked. Here's the honest verdict, because it's a near-miss rather than a clean pass:

**`rbtbc` is free today, but it sits on a reserved prefix.** It parses as `rbtb` + `c`, and `rbtb` is BCG's convention-reserved Recipe Bottle **testbench** slot — the naming table lists `«prefix»tb_testbench.sh` → `rbtb_testbench.sh` as RB's testbench file. No such file exists right now (RB's tests are Rust `rbtdt*` + fixtures, and BUK's self-test is `butt_testbench.sh`), so it's not a *live* clash — but it's the exact terminal-exclusivity ambiguity the discipline warns against: `rbtb` can't be both "the testbench" and "the parent of `rbtbc`," and `grep rbtb` (looking for the testbench) would also surface `rbtbc_` constants. So by your "no minting violations" bar, I don't think `rbtbc` quite clears it — latent, but real.

**The fix keeps your exact intent and dodges the slot: `rbtdbc_`** (consts `RBTDBC_*`).

```rust
pub const RBTDBC_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE: &str = "rbw-cC";
```

- `rbtd` + `bc` = "rbtd bash constants" — self-documenting, exactly what you were reaching for.
- Stays **under the rbtd crate cipher**, so `grep rbtd` still finds the whole crate, and it never touches the `rbtb` testbench prefix. Verified unique — no `rbtdbc` anywhere.
- One honest caveat: it's a **2-letter tail** (`bc`) where the crate's other files use RCG's single-letter classifier (`rbtdrm`, `rbtdrc`…). That's a soft-convention deviation, not a uniqueness or terminal-exclusivity violation — and it reads clearly as a generated data module, which is the regularity that matters most here.

So: `rbtbc` → latent testbench collision; `rbtdbc` → airtight, same meaning, minor classifier-length break. I'd lock **`RBTDBC_`**. Good with that, or do you want the strictly-single-letter form (`rbtdr?_` with "bash constants" in the filename/header instead of the prefix)?

content.text:
`rbtgc` has a real collision — and it's in the spec universe, so it's easy to miss.

**`rbtg` is already a populated parent:** the concept-model specs use `rbtgo_*` and `rbtgi_*` quoins extensively (`rbtgo_director_roster`, `rbtgo_ark_vouch`, `rbtgi_depot_project`, …). So `rbtg` HAS children, in the Primary Universe (AsciiDoc attributes are persistent identifiers). Minting `rbtgc_` adds a third `rbtg` child from a *different* domain — `grep rbtg` would then mix generated Rust constants with the director/depot spec quoins. Cross-universe prefix overload. Doesn't clear your bar.

(`rbtbc` → reserved testbench slot; `rbtgc` → live spec-quoin parent. The whole `rbt`-level namespace is crowded — that's the signal to drop under the crate cipher instead.)

**Clean version, same intent: `rbtdgc_`** — "rbtd **g**enerated **c**onstants", consts `RBTDGC_*`. Verified empty everywhere.

```rust
pub const RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE: &str = "rbw-cC";
```

- Under the **rbtd crate cipher**, so `grep rbtd` finds the whole crate and there's no `rbtg`/`rbtb` overload — it's cleanly in theurge's own domain, where the file physically lives.
- Same single caveat as before: `gc` is a 2-letter tail vs the crate's usual single-letter classifier — soft-convention deviation, no uniqueness/terminal violation.

So: `rbtgc` collides with the spec quoins; `rbtdgc` is airtight and reads "generated constants." Lock **`RBTDGC_`**?

# jjezs_paddock ₣BP

## Shape

Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.

The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, fixture names, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.

## Locked decisions

- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All five co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, fixtures, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
- **Full co-maintained set is in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc), fixture names (rbte). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earned a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.

## What done looks like

The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).

# jjezs_reslate ₢BPAAC

## Character

Novel BUK mechanism plus the first feeder. The design choice is settled: a shared RBK-ignorant per-pair emit primitive, colophons landing in the `RBTDGC_` file. Additive, low-risk.

## Goal

Build the shared bash→Rust const-emit primitive and wire the zipper's colophons as its first feeder, landing them in the generated `RBTDGC_` file. The primitive names nothing domain-specific; colophons are the proving case.

## Shape

- `buz_enroll` must retain the enroll varname in a new co-indexed roll — today it is `printf -v`'d into the caller and discarded, and it is the source of the const stem. Additive: `printf -v` and the existing rolls stay, callers untouched.
- Add an RBK-ignorant BUK primitive that formats one `pub const NAME: &str = "VALUE";` line from a name and value, modeled on `buz_emit_context`'s emit shape. Per-pair scalar interface — no pair-list passing (bash 3.2).
- The zipper is the first feeder: walk the co-indexed rolls; for each colophon the const name is `RBTDGC_` + the enroll varname with its `RBZ_` prefix stripped (stem verbatim), the value is the colophon string.
- Wire the generation command to write the `RBTDGC_` file and give it the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness gate the qualifier applies to the generated markdown context.

## Sources

- `Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh` — `buz_enroll` (varname discard), `buz_emit_context` (emit template), `zbuz_kindle` (roll init)
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rblm_generate` (markdown emit site to extend)
- `Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh` — the fresh-vs-committed staleness gate to mirror

## What done looks like

A varname roll exists; an RBK-ignorant per-pair emit primitive exists; the generation command writes a `rbtdgc_*` file whose colophon consts (`RBTDGC_*`) match the enrolled colophons, gated stale-on-drift. Consumers are NOT migrated here — that is the next pace. The BUK self-test is a no-regression check, not emitter verification.

# jjezs_reslate ₢BPAAD

## Character

Mechanical but broad — a consumer rename across the rbtd Rust sources.

## Goal

Migrate every colophon consumer off the hand-mirror `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*` and inline colophon literals onto the generated `RBTDGC_*` names, retiring the hand-written colophon consts. The runtime drift check stays alive here.

## Shape

- The generated colophon consts already exist (emitter pace). This pace retires the curated `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*` names and points consumers at `RBTDGC_*`.
- Discover sites with `grep -rln "RBTDRM_COLOPHON" Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/` plus the inline colophon literals (e.g. the fast suite).
- Repoint `rbtdrm_required_colophons` to the generated names but keep `rbtdrm_verify` running — do NOT retire verify here; that belongs with the diff-gate landing so drift protection is continuous.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*` mirror, `rbtdrm_verify`, `rbtdrm_required_colophons`
- the generated `rbtdgc_*` colophon consts (from the emitter pace)

## What done looks like

No `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*` consts remain; consumers and inline literals reference `RBTDGC_*`; `rbtdrm_verify` still runs against the generated consts; Rust builds and the fast suite is green.

# jjezs_reslate ₢BPAAE

## Character

RBK projector over the single-homed RBCC constants; supersedes the lib.rs path macros, the manifest credential-role mirror, and the scattered .env-filename literals. Covers the full RBCC-owned co-maintained set.

## Goal

Project every co-maintained constant that `rbcc_Constants.sh` canonically owns to generated `RBTDGC_*` consts via the shared emit primitive, retiring the hand-maintained mirrors.

## Shape

- Scope (full single-homed RBCC set): moorings dir, vessels dir, credential roles, .env filenames. Roles and .env names are hand-copied into Rust today — generation removes a live drift risk, it does not create a new home.
- Feed each as a name/value pair through the shared primitive into the `RBTDGC_` file; the const name is `RBTDGC_` + the RBCC stem (bash prefix stripped), value verbatim.
- Supersede: `rbtd_moorings_dir!` / `rbtd_vessels_dir!` macros and `RBTD_MOORINGS_DIR` in lib.rs; the credential-role consts in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`; the .env-filename literals in `rbtdrp_pristine.rs` / `rbtdrk_canonical.rs`.
- Constraint: the macro form exists because `concat!` needs a literal token, not a const identifier (see the lib.rs comment). Preserve literal-composability for any `concat!` path sites, or migrate those to runtime join.
- Note: two credential roles (assay, mason) live only in bash with no Rust mirror — projecting brings them across cleanly; confirm consumers don't already hardcode them.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbcc_Constants.sh` — flat tinder: moorings/vessels dirs, credential roles, .env filenames
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/lib.rs` — `rbtd_moorings_dir!` / `rbtd_vessels_dir!` macros
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — credential-role mirror
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrp_pristine.rs`, `rbtdrk_canonical.rs` — scattered .env-filename literals

## What done looks like

The single-homed RBCC consts are generated `RBTDGC_*`, not hand-written; the lib.rs macros, manifest role mirror, and scattered .env literals reference generation or are retired; Rust builds; moorings paths resolve unchanged; the fast suite is green.

# jjezs_reslate ₢BPAAG

## Character

Mechanical projection over a single-homed group; mirrors the colophon shape against a different bash owner.

## Goal

Generate the rbtd fixture-name consts from their canonical bash owner via the shared primitive and migrate consumers off the hand-mirror in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.

## Shape

- Fixture names are canonically authored in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` and hand-copied into `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`. Single home exists — projectable without a tidy step.
- Feed the names through the shared primitive into the `RBTDGC_` file (const name `RBTDGC_` + fixture stem); migrate manifest consumers and any inline fixture literals onto the generated consts.
- Keep the runtime drift check alive until the diff-gate lands.
